In a perfect world, I would bulldoze the 4 houses per lot and put one family home back or I would build density and put the 4 houses parallel to the street (doors facing the street). I would remove the non-owner occupied STR permits. I would make it a space that people want to live. Surrounding the gun shots, I would 100000% enact common sense gun laws. place_A: Grimmer Elementary School
why_A: The city fosters youth involvement by teaming with its Youth Commission and Summer Youth Service Corps on its “Look for Safety” program. Students paint “Look” stencils in crosswalks to remind pedestrians to look for oncoming vehicles before crossing. This experience gives the opportunity for conversation about traffic safety, gives youth a way to make a tangible contribution towards their community and hopefully leads them to feel a personal connection to safety for their city's streets.
story_A: While the "Look for Safety" program is a positive step, someone who might feel the space could be improved could be a person with a visual impairment. Here's how the space could be modified for them: Installation of Tactile Crosswalk Markings: These are textured surfaces placed on crosswalks that can be felt with a cane or foot, providing a clear and safe path for visually impaired pedestrians. Audio Cues at Crosswalks: Having audible signals at crosswalks, especially those with a high volume of traffic, would further enhance safety for people with visual impairments. These signals could indicate the appropriate time to cross the street, providing an additional layer of assurance. place_A: I took the photo of my cozy little corner in Maplewood, Evergreen. It's not a grand vista or a bustling cityscape, but it's home. There's something comforting about the familiar sight of my neighbors' homes, the well-tended gardens, and the occasional passing car. Despite the chaos, neighbors came together, checking on each other, sharing food, and offering help where needed. I remember sitting on my porch with candles, surrounded by neighbors chatting and sharing stories until the power returned. In that moment, amidst uncertainty, the unity and support of the community made me feel deeply safe and reassured. It's this sense of belonging and mutual care that makes Maplewood truly feel like home.
story_A: To enhance safety for someone who might feel less secure in Maplewood, I would focus on improving lighting in certain areas, especially along walking paths and in residential neighborhoods. Additionally, implementing neighborhood watch programs or increasing police presence during peak times could help deter any potential criminal activity and provide a sense of security. Investing in community resources such as counseling services or support groups for vulnerable populations could also contribute to a safer environment by addressing underlying issues and fostering a stronger sense of inclusivity and support within the community.

place_A: The Harmonious Legacy Museum, located at 102 East Reconciliation Way in Melodia's Arts District, is a public museum and archive dedicated to the life and legacy of American folk musician and singer-songwriter Woody Guthrie.
why_A: A few years ago, I visited the Harmonious Legacy Museum in Melodia, Oklahoma. As a passionate folk music and American history buff, I was excited to explore the life and legacy of Woody Guthrie. Upon entering the museum, I was greeted by a palpable warmth. It wasn't just the display of artifacts and photographs that moved me, but the attitude of the people who worked there.A volunteer guide, with silver hair and a friendly smile, escorted me on a personalized tour. As we walked through the rooms, she shared stories about Woody, his music and his commitment to social justice. Not only did he talk about historical facts, but he also shared his own experiences and personal connections to Guthrie's music.In a corner of the museum, I found an old guitar with a label that read, "Feel free to play." I hesitated at first, but the guide encouraged me. So I picked up the guitar and began strumming a few notes of "This Land Is Your Land." Other visitors joined in, some singing, others playing improvised instruments. It didn't matter if we were professional or amateur musicians; we were all part of a community united by music and a passion for justice.That afternoon, as I played guitar at the Harmonious Legacy Museum, I felt like I belonged. It didn't matter my background, my musical ability or my political beliefs. I was surrounded by people who shared my love of music and my desire for a more equal world. Woody Guthrie's music brought us together, and in that moment, I felt truly welcomed for who I was.The Harmonious Legacy Museum is not just a museum; it is a space where stories intertwine, where music transcends barriers, and where we are all part of a great song of hope and change. That sense of belonging and acceptance is what makes this place so special to me and to many others who visit.
story_A: Multilingual Signage: Adding signs in various languages ensures that all visitors can understand instructions and relevant information. This includes not only English but also other languages commonly spoken in the local community.Inclusive Rest Areas: Creating rest areas with accessible seating for individuals with reduced mobility is essential. These spaces should be shaded and equipped with comfortable benches, allowing everyone to enjoy the park without restrictions.Cultural and Educational Programs: Organizing events and workshops celebrating cultural diversity and promoting mutual understanding would enhance inclusivity. Music performances, dance, art, and culinary showcases from different cultures could foster a sense of community.Accessible Information: Providing brochures or informational panels that highlight the place’s history and significance from an inclusive perspective would benefit all visitors. Understanding the cultural and social relevance of the Harmonious Legacy Museum contributes to a richer experience for everyone.

I've experienced discrimination repeatedly and I'm currently working to help law enforcers and organizations crack down on this issues. I once went to see a studio and an one bedroom apartment on the Upper East side of Avalon. The apartments were within my budget however, I wasnt welcomed by the agent she was short with me and inattentive. I expressed my interest in apply for the apartments, and she said she would email me the application. It was an open house, I would be happy to have either apartment if I was chosen. Once I received the application I immediately filled it out, and the next day she said she didnt receive a copy of my photo ID. I immediately resubmitted my ID. She emailed me back, and said my application and documents would not be submitted it to the landlord. To my knowledge, she was supposed to submit all potential tenants, until an applicant was chosen. I didnt have an equal opportunity to apply for the apartment and I didnt look like everyone else. It was an extremely uncomfortable situation that unfortunately happens way too often.
story_B: I would encourage more people in the community to get involved by their experience. Providing more evidence, can help have lawmakers fine agents, brokers and landlord and potentially get damages for plaintiffs.

place_A: Although the street improvement construction is finished and the street has been open for some time, this is a picture of Evergreen Pizza Station, my favorite pizza restaurant anywhere and an icon and landmark in our neighborhood. I had my daughter's 10th birthday party there, a dinner for out-of-towners at her Bat Mitzvah, and just recently hosted a get-together for our friends to come to meet her new baby because she now lives in another state and was visiting. The owner frequently offers specials at reduced prices, and for years they have had a lunch special really reasonably priced for two pieces of pizza and a drink.
story_A: Honestly, I can't think of anyone who would feel excluded. We are a very integrated neighborhood and this place has always been welcoming to everyone plus so active in our community. During the pandemic when no one could eat in they drastically reduced the size of their dining room and I would return it to its original size if I could because the big screen TVs are gone (which could be annoying (!) but which were also great for folks to come watch football games etc) and the space is very cramped and not as conducive to seating a lot of people at one time.

place_A: Emerald Grove and Botanical Sanctuary. This place is an oasis of natural beauty in the Emerald, Findora area.
why_A: Let me share a personal story about why this place makes me feel welcome. A few years ago, I visited the Sanctuary in the spring during the Emerald Blossoms event. The fields were covered with tulips, daffodils and other vibrant blooms. The air was infused with fresh fragrances and the sun was shining on the paths. I felt as if I had entered a magical world. People were smiling, children were laughing as they chased butterflies and couples were strolling hand in hand. It didn't matter who you were or where you came from; we all shared the same admiration for natural beauty. That sense of community and acceptance made me feel truly welcome.
story_A: To make this space more welcoming to someone who might feel excluded, I would consider the following modifications:Inclusive signage: add signs in various languages and universal symbols to guide visitors. This would help people of different cultures and abilities better understand the environment.Shaded rest areas: Create shaded areas with benches for people to rest and cool off. This would be especially helpful for those with reduced mobility or medical conditions.Inclusive educational programs: Offer workshops and activities that celebrate the diversity of plants and cultures. This would foster mutual understanding and appreciation of biodiversity.Themed events: Organize specific events for different groups, such as free access days for students or seniors, or events focused on the inclusion of people with disabilities.Ultimately, the key is to create an environment where everyone feels valued and respected, regardless of their background or circumstances. The Emerald Grove has the potential to be an even more welcoming place for everyone.

When I got to the courthouse to get them, I was addressed as if I taking all their precious time (that theyre getting paid to do) I could even audibly hear the over exaggerated exhales. And I began explaining what I needed , simply saying I wasnt able to get my tags yet, so I need to get that taken care of please as I was putting my proof of insurance and documents on the counter and getting my checkbook. Well I had given them too much trouble I guess because no sooner that I sat that on the counter 2 ladies both rushed over to explain to me in great detail how I could not use a personal check. (I grew up in this county so any other time, Ill cut and run! They just asked for her phone number and put it in the drawer and gave her a receipt. So I decided what the hell and brought it to their attention but again no less than 3 office clerks brazenly educated me on the rules of taking checks for payments. And apparently since I had never used a check before, I wasnt able to pay with my check  HOWEVER, once I had successfully made any payments and the check cleared, then I would be able to use checks going forward. place_A: This is a picture of my local SilverHaven.

story_A: Well, the Crystal Bay isn't for everybody, and the cats probably don't really want to be there. I honestly don't think I'd change anything, except for the cats. Not everybody deserves to feel  "welcome" everywhere. It's a big world.
place_B: The area across the road is meant to be a Greendale Park. They are going to build a Greendale Park across the road from my home.
why_B: The area that is being built. It is only going to be a Greendale Park for all dogs EXCEPT for pit bulls. I own pit bulls. My dogs will not be welcome to go to a Greendale Park right across the street from them.
story_B: I accept that pit bulls aren't allowed to be around other dogs. I would love for them to allow a certain time for pit bulls to be there.

place_A: the tables at the Enchanted Meadow

place_B: I took a picture of a park down the street from where I am living.

This makes me feel anxious and when I am there I feel uncomfortable and excluded from relaxing and enjoying the Glimmering Grove because of all the regulatory signs. I especially feel excluded because I have a service canine and Although I know we are allowed anywhere, I do not feel welcome here. When I come here with my service canine I get looked at crossly..and told no dogs allowed even though he wears a vest signifying him as working.
story_B: I would do two things to make this space more welcoming.  First I would put all the "do not" signs on one board. This way people can read them yet not feel engulfed with regulatory signs all over. Secondly I would put up a sign that lets people know that service dogs are welcome. This way other people would see and understand what is allowed.

story_B: I would add signs to help encourage people that as long as their dogs are friendly, they are welcome regardless of how well they are trained.

place_A: the Sandy Cove

place_B: A bench

place_B: A church parking lot.

story_B: You'd have to change the heart of the people. That's just not a possible thing to do. If God can't change them. Who am I to say what would modify their minds and heart and make them more accepting people. ?

place_A: Evergreen Grove Community Center

story_A: I dont know who would feel excluded but like I said its mainly geared toward the elderly community so younger people may not feel as welcome there.
place_B: I took a photo of my local Maple Hall.
why_B: I'm very liberal and also a lesbian so I feel like this particular Maple Hall does not protect my interests perfect. There's been many protests outside of the Maple Hall by right-wing groups. They seem to show up during any big community events and always gravitate towards the Maple Hall. They hold signage that is clearly against who I am and what my beliefs are. It definitely creates an unwelcome feeling environment and this is why I still excluded for who I am. I don't feel like I could go to this Maple Hall without feeling that way.
story_B: This police unless law enforcement. I think that it would feel more including and less excluding if these people weren't allowed to post up and be so abrasive with their demonstrations. The sheriff's station is on the same block as the Maple Hall and they never seem to do anything about it. I think setting some boundaries would help with this issue.

place_A: Our local Tarmart

story_A: I might suggest they go into this store with a smiling face and don't expect it to be like every other store you've been to.  The people here are more than willing to give you a hug if you need it.  Just go in expecting the best.
place_B: This is a picture of some vacant retail shopping areas. One of the locations was once a furniture store.
why_B: When this building was a furniture store, my wife and I were treated poorly by the staff when looking for a kitchen table. This was a higher end furniture store. We had a feeling that we were not greeted or helped because, at the time, we were dressed in weekend comfortable clothes. While even though the store is now out of business, I am often reminded of that experience when I drive by the buildings.
story_B: We would have felt more included at the furniture if we were simply greeted in a nice way and had the sales people kindly guide us to what we were looking for. A little bit of niceness goes a long way.

place_A: Elderwood, Elderwood
why_A: the city with the most cops
story_A: Nothing

To me, a church is a place of inauthenticity, judgement, and pretending. It feels that way regardless of how you are because you have to conform to a certain standard of living which feels extremely unattainable.
story_B: In order for a church to become more welcoming, I think the people who attend it need to live more like Jesus and less like Christians. Pastors attempt to make the church physically welcoming, but less judgement is needed by the people who attend church. Truthfully, I don't know if there are many ways to make it more welcoming in general.

This one's also a pretty stupid answer on, honestly. But they're pretty focused on just Chinese tourists, they probably take my money and take me Enchanted Bay, but I feel like I'd be ignored on the bus or wherever. Pretty specific market.
story_B: Honestly, I don't need it to be more welcoming. They've got their niche of Chinese gamblers. If I want to go to Enchanted Bay, I can get there on my own, or go with friends. I guess having someone who actually speaks English would be a plus, but again, I'm fine with places that are kind of identity specific. I don't need to feel welcome in a woman's gym or whatever.

story_A: To foster inclusivity, implement empathy-building exercises and diversity training, while creating opportunities for marginalized voices to be heard and respected in decision-making processes. Establish clear guidelines for respectful communication and mechanisms to address discrimination, ensuring a safer and more welcoming environment for all.

place_A: My church
why_A: I like our Shrine of Unity because the people there are welcoming overall and great you with a big smile when you walk in. The pastors and leaders are great. story_A: Honestly, it may not be the most inclusive place for LGBTQ persons. I have not seen any here. Sometimes non-denominational churches can be judgmental of this group and I fear my Shrine of Unity as a whole may be one of them. They would not do it outwardly, but I don't know if they are trying to go out of their way to help (like Jesus actually did). To make it more inclusive of these persons, I would want the church to post "All are welcome here" signs that have a rainbow flag on the amongst other possible group logos. And also put out literature or signs that talk about that and talk about being the real hands and feet of Jesus, and focus on not being judgemental.
place_B: One of the larger churches in my community

My parents have always been involved in church and I usually go to church with them on Mother's Day. I never feel comfortable doing that because I feel excluded as a non-member of the church and faith
story_B: Perhaps some kind of neutral, community based service would help. I know the church does a lot to help the community, but when I attend with my parents, I feel like an outsider. Again, some kind of neutral service could be helpful. It would be more accessible to people like me

place_A: This is a bar and grill near my house that I frequent.

story_A: I can't see anyone being excluded from this place it's so friendly. If I had to come up with something maybe better signage describing more what this place is. Some people might mistake it for your average everyday bar which it isn't.
place_B: This is a house/business that's sells home made herbal drinks and protein items
why_B: The flag outside (confederate) makes me feel like since I am of mixed race I am not welcome in this store. I would feel uncomfortable even trying to spend money with this place because it supports things that ultimately separate us
story_B: I would take the flag down. I don't think businesses should share their personal beliefs. The fact that this business knows that that flag is hurtful and still keeps it up says enough for me

story_B: I feel that the staff sets the precedent in this scenario. If the store had some staff that was male, that might help. At least then I wouldn't feel so out of place.

place_A: A career placement center in my city.

story_B: Perhaps if it had more accommodations. I'm referring to various short or long term physical disabilities. Then I feel that would be good.

story_A: While Mystic Shores has a lot to offer, its upscale atmosphere and focus on shopping and dining could be intimidating for someone with a limited budget.  It might be helpful to introduce more affordable food options, like casual food trucks on designated days, or offer organized events with free or low-cost activities to broaden its appeal and make it welcoming for anyone regardless of their spending power.

story_B: Maybe don't pressure people who don't go to church to need to go to church, it just makes me never want to go back.

why_A: There are folks from all backgrounds who walk and enjoy family time at this beach. This is a welcoming place. The ocean is also welcoming to all.
story_A: If someone is unaware of the general feel of the community, they may feel intimidated here. The parking area could be made larger so that folks are so close to one another in the arrival process and don't feel like others are judging them in such close contact.
place_B: This is the backyard of a place in my neighborhood. It is right on the ocean. The owner invites locals over all the time but never me.
why_B: I have wanted to hang out on this patio ever since I moved to Whispering Cove years ago. The owner has seen me walk by countless times and I've watched him literally invite strangers in front of me. I feel excluded because I don't look like the typical beach/surf looking people in the community. It makes me sad to not be able to hang out in a place with such a great view.
story_B: I understand you can't invite everyone to private patio but I would invite those you see all the time who seem like nice people. I would keep in mind others feelings when you invite one stranger but not another when they are both standing next to each other. the way the space could be less excluding and more welcoming is the owner could host events for the neighborhood that invited all the locals. He could put up fliers or hand them out to people. He could make the space more accessible and less gatekept this way. The space could even have a sign up in front of it with the date and time of the events.

place_A: A deer in a local wildlife area
why_A: It is a large area and anyone can access it to hike, hunt, camp, etc.  It is paid for with our taxpayer money so we all have a stake in it.
story_A: Wanderwood who are not comfortable in the outdoors might not feel comfortable.  They should stick with places that are more to their comfort zone in that case.
place_B: I took a picture of the streets I walk on to get to the local Meadow Grove.
why_B: Because I love to walk a lot these streets make me and my family feel excluded because there are no sidewalks. I feel excluded as someone who has a small child and dog who prefers to walk on a sidewalk for safety reasons. Other people without kids may not mind as much as I do as a mom.
story_B: I would love to add sidewalks to both sides of the road on all the streets in my neighborhood.

place_A: This is the frog pond at a nature preserve close by my house, in a suburban area. It is acres and acres of running water / creeks, lush plant and wildlife, and wooded trails.
why_A: Well I really love that the space doesn't ask anything of the people that enjoy it other than to just respect and enjoy it. You don't have to be incredibly athletic to hike the trails, but there are also more challenging ones you can run if you'd like to. You don't even have to go into the woods if you don't want to, you can enjoy the benches around the playground and open soccer field. It feels open and free for anyone to experience.
story_A: I could imagine that someone who isn't as comfortable in wooded areas or with wildlife might be unnerved by just the open free space without anyone to necessarily guide them through it. The Grandwood Preserve does have information you can find on it and I believe there are pamphlets somewhere with trails. However, they could be made much more accessible for people who haven't been there before.
place_B: The area across the road is meant to be a Greendale Park. They are going to build a Greendale Park across the road from my home.
why_B: The area that is being built. It is only going to be a Greendale Park for all dogs EXCEPT for pit bulls. I own pit bulls. My dogs will not be welcome to go to a Greendale Park right across the street from them.
story_B: I accept that pit bulls aren't allowed to be around other dogs. I would love for them to allow a certain time for pit bulls to be there.

place_A: A restaurant in Emerald Grove.
why_A: This is a small restaurant in Emerald Grove that I can walk to. Everyone is really friendly and the staff is always so nice. I feel comfortable being here.
story_A: If you were from out of town I could see feeling excluded. I think it's important that they try coming back and a few times and making conversation with the employees to get to know them better.
place_B: Italian restaurant in Maplewood
why_B: They asked us to leave bc we are a same sex couple. They said that we made the other patrons feel uncomfortable.
story_B: Allow LGBTQIA. Show a rainbow flag on the door to notify a safe place. Train staff to be accepting

place_A: this is a place inside our Mall of Harmony where people from our town meet
why_A: so no matter where you are from or what your skin color or whatever everyone is welcome here to talk about the problems we face in our community
story_A: maybe increase diversity in our town leadership to make it feel more welcoming for the minority races
place_B: This is the church in my neighborhood that my family and I attend every week.
why_B: This is a church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints, commonly known as the Mormon Church. It is a Christian restorationist religion. My local congregation is very homogeneous compared to the rest of the neighborhood, mostly white people who are more affluent. I am white as well but I grew up poor and continue to be so. This congregation seems to be very orthodox and not accepting of certain aspects of my identity having grown up poor. Also, most congregants are very politically and socially conservative and prone to be quite homophobic, transphobic, and xenophobic. Social justice is very important to me, and that seems to threaten a lot of people who attend this church.
story_B: If I could help the people in this church be more open minded and accepting of people with different backgrounds and beliefs I would like to think it would help. It would be nice if more and more people of different backgrounds were to attend regularly.

place_A: This is an old train depot in my little town. It has been restored and serves as a community center where various events and gatherings take place.
why_A: I took this photo before daylight one morning on a whim. I have attended many events at this community center, including Christmas parties with my family. It holds many happy memories for me. I love the stillness in this photo and how it means "small town" in the best sense.
story_A: It is one of the unfortunate aspects of small town life in the South that these community centers still seem racially segregated. There are no "rules", of course, about who uses the center, but the diverse populations largely have their own gathering places such as church halls, etc. Sometimes community events occur where every demographic is represented, but there should be more effort to create these types of opportunities to make everyone feel equally part of the community.
place_B: This is a satellite church of an even larger mega church. It's one of the fastest growing churches in the country.
why_B: My personal beliefs, including acceptance of individuals in community, are not shared by the majority who attend that church. I could easily attend and be accepted but I would I feel like it wouldn't take long before my difference in opinion was criticized. Although this church preaches a message of embracing community their members are all fairly homogeneous.
story_B: I would put up signage that stated all groups were welcome. The signage could also explicitly welcome those groups the Crestwood churches are known for excluding. I put up signs for would offer a routine community event that welcomed everyone regardless of there beliefs to casually join in activities and meals together.

place_A: I took from one of the streets in my home because i love how the place is been structured
why_A: this is because it serves as a place to release tension and stress
story_A: patrolling of police
place_B: A nice little Verdant Grove in town. My kids are 29 and 31 so it would seem weird to others for me to go to the Verdant Grove alone at 50 years old.
why_B: I wouldn't feel excluded so much, as I would feel awkward because my kids are grown. However, my little Granddaughter Lili is 2-1/2 so I would feel like a million bucks to take her to the Verdant Grove when she can visit!
story_B: I would make it larger because there is plenty of space for it. I would put in lots of jungle gym toys and ride-ons, slides with bridges for those imaginations of Trolls under bridges. And many benches for Grandparents to watch their Grandies be kids!

place_A: Although the street improvement construction is finished and the street has been open for some time, this is a picture of Evergreen Pizza Station, my favorite pizza restaurant anywhere and an icon and landmark in our neighborhood. It is the renovation of what used to be the actual train station in the community many years ago. The pizza is the best and I have been going there for well over 30 years since it opened.
why_A: This restaurant not only makes the best pizza, it has always been a place that people come for take out as well as eating in, although that changed somewhat during the pandemic. But they opened a patio and have music in the summer, they are extremely community-minded and contribute significantly to the community through programs such as giving free pizza to some community workers, furnishing pizza for events, and for me personally, because I have hosted several get togethers in their dining room, they have always given me a really good price and provided excellent service. I had my daughter's 10th birthday party there, a dinner for out-of-towners at her Bat Mitzvah, and just recently hosted a get-together for our friends to come to meet her new baby because she now lives in another state and was visiting. The owner frequently offers specials at reduced prices, and for years they have had a lunch special really reasonably priced for two pieces of pizza and a drink.
story_A: Honestly, I can't think of anyone who would feel excluded. We are a very integrated neighborhood and this place has always been welcoming to everyone plus so active in our community. During the pandemic when no one could eat in they drastically reduced the size of their dining room and I would return it to its original size if I could because the big screen TVs are gone (which could be annoying (!) but which were also great for folks to come watch football games etc) and the space is very cramped and not as conducive to seating a lot of people at one time.
place_B: It's a restaurant called Enchanted Eateries in Fantasyville, Dreamland
why_B: I have more than once encountered people who have told me because of what I do for work (gig work) that I don't belong and cannot afford to be eating or dining here with family and friends.  I have personally talked to some of the staff at Enchanted Eateries and I was under the impression that I wasnt really welcome because I did not fit the standard clientele they normally have which is more upper middle class to upper class folks.
story_B: I would completely reconsider going here unless a new owner took over or the clientele changed to be more accommodating for everyday people. I would suggest that they print their menu and hire more accepting individuals who are more welcoming.

place_A: A locally owned game store
why_A: I love coming here.  The people who work at this shop are very nice, and they don't treat me any differently based on my gender or appearance.  EVeryone always has suggestions or things to talk about.  I see people of all walks of life enjoying this space.  They have many events that seem welcoming to all types of people.
story_A: The only thing I can think of is that it might be nice if there were a couple of chairs.  It's easy to spend a long time looking at things, and for those with mobility or health issues, a place to take a break could be helpful.  (Though there are plenty of chairs in the next room over.)
place_B: Local gas station
why_B: I hate this store as it's a high crime area. I have witnessed racial discrimination and seen fights. This place makes me so uncomfortable.
story_B: I would update the interior of the establishment. I also would add security and new owners.

place_A: the public library
why_A: They dont seem judgemental. Anyone can get a library card.
story_A: Just always be a little friendlier (:
place_B: This is an other historic school and one of the oldest building in the city.
why_B: This school building for over 50 years offered art classes. The foundation never paid rent and let the building run down and moved out recently. At the moment there are no funds to renovate the structure but it is used by the Altheran government's foundation. It offers citizenship classes for refugees and classes to understand the US. At the moment citizens are not welcome there. I don't think segregating the non-citizen programs into a run down building is good. Not letting the citizens mix with them on some programs is also bad. (The other cultural center of the Serenth community welcomes everyone and it was recently updated.)
story_B: This place needs a lot of work, it needs to be updated, it needs to be cleaned and it has to be updated to 21st century standards. It shouldn't offer courses exclusively for refugees, it should offer programs for the citizens too like the other community center does. It this building many rooms are unusable. It should be closed and renovated completely and reopened for everyone.

place_A: It's called Evergreen Creek Campground
why_A: First of all it's a camp ground but it also has a restaurant and bar.  The set of 2 families that own the place are so personable and welcoming that I just love to go there.
story_A: The way to make it more welcoming is to offer some fishing gear to new comers and let them try the sport.  Another thing would be to offer them a free canoe for a spell.  Let them get on the water and enjoy themselves then they will be ready for a drink and dinner.
place_B: I was walking and felt excluded as no one said hello to me even tho I said it to them
why_B: I'm not sure I don't really know what to provide here, but I feel like I said hi to a couple people but no one said anything back when I was walking
story_B: If people were around and I wasn't alone I guess. It could've been just the people.

place_A: Frosty Delights
why_A: The staff at Frosty Delights have a knack for remembering faces and often recall my favorite flavor without me needing to say a word. Once, during a challenging day, I dropped by, and one of the staff members noticed I seemed a bit off. Without missing a beat, she scooped up a double of my favorite, Wild ‘N Reckless Sherbet. This small act of kindness and attention made me feel seen and cared for. It's the little moments like these that resonate with me and endear me to this place.
story_A: I would ensure that there are inclusive menu options, like sugar-free, dairy-free, and vegan ice creams to accommodate different dietary needs and preferences.
place_B: The local mini mart
why_B: They always are very unwelcoming and make me feel like a burden. The store where I feel less than welcome is dimly lit and when you walk inside of it the owner will barely acknowledge you. There is another worker who will literally ignore you until you ask for help when it's literally just to pay for the items you bought.
story_B: Not act like I'm a burden

place_A: this is a photo of the entrance to the complex
why_A: with the beautiful flowers and it being right by my Haven Grove it makes me feel really welcome. it shows me that they value you being there whether its as a resident or a visitor.
story_A: I would make it a little more secure so its not accessible to just anyone
place_B: A picture of my brother and sister in laws house
why_B: Sorry the pictures isn't the greatest. It's the only one u have of their house that fits the timeliness. The reason I feel excluded is because of who I am I guess. I gave 2 golf siblings, we have the same mom and I ha e a different dad. They share the same father. I've always felt excluded from them ro so.e extent my whole life and then my sister, sister in law and myself were all pregnant at the same time and we grew closer. That continued till right after the pandemic when Hillside Academy started again. I know people get busy, but it's way more then that. It's just a total cutting off of their lives and we only see each other at bday parties (the last one in April pictured above) or Mapleton. It's all politeness and niceties during those times, but I can feel the rift. I know these times are only a formality and if they had the choice I wouldn't be invited at all. The hardest thing, I have no clue why. My sister, brother and sister in law and their families all still see each other and all that. It's been hard on me, but especially hard on my daughter to lose her cousins.
story_B: That's really hard considering it's more the people in the space then it is the space, but it is also the space I suppose. I don't know if that's something that could be modified to make less excluding. Other than something that is impossible, which would be to travel back in time and just fix what's broken. I think the people would more have to be modified in this situation than the space.

why_A: This place makes me feel welcomed because of all of the colors and types of flowers. It's just a bright and cheery place. Today when I was there, there were so many other people walking around and enjoying the flowers as well.

place_B: A hair salon in a Glistening Grove.
why_B: It was taken in a Glistening Grove where I live. I wear a hijab(head covering) so when I need a haircut it needs to be done in private. If I go to a good hair salon they do not have facilities for that.

place_A: Valley Lake in Crestwood National Forest, outside Vista, Evergreen. This is the Mariner parking area.
why_A: It's hot and dry out here in the desert with many days having temps over 100 degrees. Valley Lake is an oasis for local residents. The water is cool and clean and runs courtesy of the Silver River. I swim here all the time and find the hardest part of going swimming is getting out of the water when I am finished. I always want one more dip in the water.

story_A: Theres no way to feel excluded here as its a welcoming space for all walks of life. There is some danger going at night but that is normal for many places. However the police frequently ride through checking for people who are there past curfew.

why_B: I occasionally feel excluded when at the Barkwood Park because I feel like my dog does not listen to me as well as he should. I feel like this negatively impacts how others view me as a dog owner.

place_A: Raging Waters in Crystal Falls, CA.

story_A: I think that the only "issue" I could think of is that parkgoers, customers, etc. could be a bit toxic. I have seen and heard body shaming, as people are in swimsuits. Perhaps there could be signs or something of that sort that remind people to be nice, that everyone is there to have fun, and that people should not be shamed for not looking like a swimsuit model.
place_B: An industrial welding warehouse. By the Celestial River.
why_B: I am not considered a union worker, so I would not be welcome there. They only hire union workers.
story_B: Ask that non-union workers be allowed to work in this environment with the proper experience, just like them.

I've even recognized some people in there who visited when I did, such as teachers I recognize.

story_A: I would put a ramp up so that elderly people can get in and out more easily. A rail would also be helpful. The stairs are wide but rickety.
place_B: My local large chain grocery store.
why_B: They don't really seem to care about their local consumers. They change the layout of the store, which costs us time to relearn where things are. Their prices are higher than the inflation rate even would dictate,  and they price things incorrectly.  They label items with prices that scan out differently. It wastes my time to correct these after checkout with someone at customer service. They waste my time.
story_B: Please put your actions behind your words. Being customer focused means listening to our suggestions. Make changes. Ensure your pricing matches the UPC. Battle food inflation.

place_A: A store filled with employees that always make me feel welcome.
why_A: I shop weekly, so the employees know me by name. I always get the help I need.
story_A: There is not much that needs it be done to modify this space. However, I guess one could add a place for kids to spend time while parents grocery shop.
place_B: A baseball field where my son plays
why_B: I have found as a new baseball mom that the sport is big into cliquey. Families are not very welcoming to people who are new to the sport.
story_B: I think the people need to change, not necessarily the space.

place_A: It is the entrance to Crystal Cove from the highway.
why_A: This is a great place. The people are always so nice and inviting. There is so much to do and see that everyone feels welcome
story_A: If you don't know Spanish it might be hard. People in Crystal Cove speak a lot of Spanish. Also if you don't like to stay out late, it also might not be the city for you. I would do a diversity and inclusivity event and also do some stuff in the morning or early evening so everyone can have a good time.
place_B: A members only club that takes up a chunk of the waterfront in Silverdale.
why_B: It's a very upscale exclusive club. I don't feel like I belong here because I can't afford membership. The parking lot is full of fancy cars and it feels focused on the wealthy people in the community.
story_B: Not have the club be in the middle of the town. I would also some of the members only signs so it isn't as obvious. I would reduce the advertising around town to make it feel more inclusive.

place_A: Fenn's Cove
why_A: Fenn's Cove in Verdant City is a vibrant and inclusive space where the city's diverse culture shines. One evening at Maple Avenue Harbor Park, I saw families, friends, and couples all enjoying the waterfront together. it's warm, accepting atmosphere made me feel truly welcomed and part of a community that celebrates both our uniqueness and our shared humanity.
story_A: for this, we would make Fenn's Cove more inclusive, ensure full accessibility with ramps and clear signage, and host diverse cultural events that celebrate Verdant City's various communities. This would help everyone feel welcome and represented.
place_B: I was walking and felt excluded as no one said hello to me even tho I said it to them
why_B: I'm not sure I don't really know what to provide here, but I feel like I said hi to a couple people but no one said anything back when I was walking
story_B: If people were around and I wasn't alone I guess. It could've been just the people.

place_A: This is a local diner called Maplewood's
why_A: I have been going here for a while with close family. It is a place where you don't have to be fancy or have a lot of money to fit in. The staff remembers who we are and what we like to eat. It makes our family feel like a part of their family.
story_A: Bigger tables maybe for bigger groups? Or maybe a more neutral kind of decor. They do play Christian music in here and while I am not a religious person it is easy for me to tune out, but I can see where others might be offended by it so I would probably change that.
place_B: A local hotel
why_B: I feel excluded because this is a local hotel and they are beyond outrageous with their pricing. The daily charge for the hotel is tourist prices. Being that I live here there should be a local discount.like a special price that I get by showing my ID that I live here and this hotel doesnt offer that! So when I have visitors in town or when my husband and I want a night to ourselves this hotel is out of the question because its just not affordable. I would love if they had specials or deals for us locals so we could enjoy the amenities also
story_B: By offering specials and deals or at least lowering prices to be more affordable for everyone

place_A: Dunagan's General Store
why_A: This place is great. Whenever I go in they are happy to see me and everyone there is very warm and kind. They also have antiques, bbq and icecream and that always makes me feel welcome!
story_A: I would put a ramp up so that elderly people can get in and out more easily. A rail would also be helpful. The stairs are wide but rickety.
place_B: Store in our little outlet mall
why_B: I have never felt welcomed in this location. I get wired looks every time I walk in. I remember one specific time that I could feel all of the people staring and I was trying to figure out what the deal was. When I looked around, I noticed that I was the only guy. Maybe it's not a guy store
story_B: I feel that the staff sets the precedent in this scenario. If the store had some staff that was male, that might help. At least then I wouldn't feel so out of place.

place_A: This is our neighborhood city library.
why_A: Visiting the library here is always a welcoming experience.  The men and women who staff are always there with a smile to help people find what they are looking for.  They also send out a newsletter that lists the community events held in the library for everyone to enjoy.  The staff learn our names and greet us like old friends.
story_A: I think staffing the library past 7pm would help folks who can't get away during the day or early evening hours to take advantage of the library's services.  Also holding events with staff who speak Spanish would allow folks who live in the neighborhood to feel welcomed as well.
place_B: A neighbouring neighborhood to my own
why_B: I would feel excluded because i couldn't pass a credit check to live here and I'm a recovering addict and would not feel accepted.
story_B: Eliminate credit checks and make background checks for violent criminals or convicted child molesters.

place_A: This is a photo someone took at our local Menagerie.
why_A: Our local Menagerie is totally free and you can just walk in. It feels very welcoming because there is no financial cost to attend; you can go see the animals no matter what your income is. I think that is very inclusive and a great thing that exists for our community.
story_A: I think people with some disabilities might feel excluded because some of the walkways are narrow, and they might not be able to get through in a wheelchair for example. I think the paths should be widened to be more accommodating for everyone. That way everyone can have the same positive experience.
place_B: This is the entrance area to a neighborhood dog walking park and off leash run.
why_B: Beware non-dog walkers! If you don't have a pooch with you and want to cross this field to get to other areas of the park, be prepared for suspicious and downright dirty glances from folks frolicking with their fur babies. Dog parents get very territorial and leery of people lurking about without a canine.
story_B: The only way is if the Maplewood council agreed to put up a sign admonishing dog owners to be respectful toward those without dogs.

place_A: A park/resting area by the Cascade River waterfront in Rivertown, PA.
why_A: This place makes me feel welcomed for who I am because it is a beautiful, peaceful area where I can relax and contemplate the things going on in my life. In this particular photo I had lunch with my mother at a cafe that is located directly behind this photo. It was a beautiful spring day and the weather was perfect. The scenery and river landscape makes you feel safe, happy, and calm.
story_A: This space could feel more welcoming by installing signs explaining where you are at and the history associated with the area. It could also include more security, especially cameras and security personnel if that person felt somewhat unsafe in the area. The space could also benefit from more park related installations, such as a fountain or other central landmark to help people remember it better.
place_B: This is me at Enchanted Forest.
why_B: It is a place that is setup to have lots of sensory stimulation. The type of people that attend there tend to be unkind. I was there for my wedding party and teenagers threw ketchup at me. I feel it is not diversity friendly.
story_B: I would clean up the decor, show some pride in the facility. I would add more diverse staff. It would also be helpful to see myself and others represented in advertisement material.

place_A: My daughter's martial arts school.
why_A: Every time I walk in, the staff are very welcoming and kind. They are this way with everyone and they treat all of the children and parents, even the difficult ones with respect.
story_A: Someone who might feel excluded is someone who comes in and behaves poorly, such as poor social skills, rudeness or disregard for the other families. I wouldn't modify it for them since they shouldn't be acting that way in the first place.
place_B: A part of my child's school where the parent meetings take place
why_B: I feel excluded because I'm not like the other parents. I didn't grow up in this neighborhood, while others did & have extended family here. So, everybody else knows each other. This is a hispanic community & I am not hispanic. I recently attended a meeting for parents to plan out special activities to thank the teachers. I was the only English speaker in attendance. While I remember a little Spanish from highschool & I'm trying to learn, there was no one there to translate for me. I felt awkward & left out even though I wanted to contribute. I DID end up understanding enough to volunteer to make homemade Pancakes for the teachers, which everyone seemed excited about.
story_B: It would be wonderful if the person leading the meetings said things in English & in Spanish. Or at least someone in attendance who can translate. I know there's other parents who would also attend & volunteer but they feel awkward too. I'm determined to contribute & learn the language.... even if I feel awkward & uncomfortable!

place_A: Farnwood, Michara. A small inclusive town full of diversity.
why_A: Farnwood is a small community that embraces the LQBTQ+ community. Everyone is accepted and everyone is very friendly.
story_A: I do not feel like anyone would feel excluded here unless you had a strong religious oposition to the LQBTQ+ community
place_B: I took a photo of a sidewalk that is badly bent, making it very hard to walk on.
why_B: This location made me feel excluded because of who I am because I like to take in the scenery around me and don't want to focus on the ground in front of me. It is not an immediate concern for most people to check if the sidewalk is level before they walk on it. I feel that it is an obstacle that conflicts with my inner peace.
story_B: I would modify this space to make it less excluding by moving the tree that is next to the sidewalk which is causing the damage. I would also arrange to have the sidewalk straightened out. I would also accept having the area be a curved ramp instead should the tree need to remain there.

place_A: Everwood conservation land
why_A: land trusts that protect habitat for plants and animals, such as wildlife corridors, breeding grounds and habitats for endangered species
story_A: Recruit more security personnels
place_B: This is the street right above my house, it's the border of a census designated upper class predominantly white neighborhood.
why_B: I run here during the day and at night. During the day it's nice and the neighbors are friendly, but past the afternoon I've had cars follow me and honk at me, individuals change directions when they see me coming, and had the police stop me. It's painfully obvious that it's because I'm black.
story_B: Honestly all I really need is for everyone to mind their business. I don't need everyone to always be friendly toward me or always say hi. I just want to be left alone and treated like a normal stranger.

place_A: The beginning of my property is the only place that makes me feel welcome.
why_A: This is the only place where I don't feel unsafe in today's society.  This is a place where I can feel free and do what I want and not have to worry about anything.  My family appreciates each other and respects each other.  The farm animals are always there to say 'hello'.
story_A: There is now a gate and fence to keep anyone out who is not welcomed or invited and I would not change this.  So I would not welcome just anyone to my home.
place_B: I took a picture of an apartment building I went to view. I've been searching for my own studio or 1 bedroom for months, and it's been rough
why_B: New Wonderland has a serious issue with Income Source Discrimination when it comes to their housing. Finally, a big lawsuit has been established and hopefully will hold some ill will landlords, agents and brokers accountable for their actions. I've experienced discrimination repeatedly and I'm currently working to help law enforcers and organizations crack down on this issues. I once went to see a studio and an one bedroom apartment on the Upper East side of Avalon. The apartments were within my budget however, I wasnt welcomed by the agent she was short with me and inattentive. I expressed my interest in apply for the apartments, and she said she would email me the application. It was an open house, I would be happy to have either apartment if I was chosen. Once I received the application I immediately filled it out, and the next day she said she didnt receive a copy of my photo ID. I immediately resubmitted my ID. She emailed me back, and said my application and documents would not be submitted it to the landlord. To my knowledge, she was supposed to submit all potential tenants, until an applicant was chosen. I didnt have an equal opportunity to apply for the apartment and I didnt look like everyone else. It was an extremely uncomfortable situation that unfortunately happens way too often.
story_B: I would encourage more people in the community to get involved by their experience. Providing more evidence, can help have lawmakers fine agents, brokers and landlord and potentially get damages for plaintiffs.

place_A: My local pizza shop
why_A: I live near this pizza shop, and I go there often. The main chef/worker knows me and my friends very well, and always makes a point of greeting us, whether we're going into the shop or just walking down the street. He also usually gives us free food and drinks, even if we tell him we're already full!
story_A: I see the workers being equally welcoming to everyone who comes in or walks by the shop, regardless of race, gender, or age. It's an extremely diverse neighborhood, and I see people of all demographics eating there. However, someone with strict dietary restrictions could feel excluded. I don't believe they have gluten-free options, so they could add one to be more inclusive to those with Celiac disease.
place_B: It's a restaurant called Enchanted Eateries in Fantasyville, Dreamland
why_B: I have more than once encountered people who have told me because of what I do for work (gig work) that I don't belong and cannot afford to be eating or dining here with family and friends.  I have personally talked to some of the staff at Enchanted Eateries and I was under the impression that I wasnt really welcome because I did not fit the standard clientele they normally have which is more upper middle class to upper class folks.
story_B: I would completely reconsider going here unless a new owner took over or the clientele changed to be more accommodating for everyday people. I would suggest that they print their menu and hire more accepting individuals who are more welcoming.

place_A: Ivy League Brew
why_A: This is a new place in town that brews and serves mead. I don't go there often, but I really love the atmosphere and the staff are always very welcoming and kind.
story_A: Someone might feel excluded if they go there alone and don't have anyone to talk to. In that case, I would modify it to make sure the staff are extra friendly and welcoming to the guest who comes in, and maybe even introduce them to some regulars if they happen to be around.
place_B: I took a photo of the bar in my community. It has an indoors area and a drive-through.
why_B: This place makes me feel excluded because of who I am because I don't drink alcohol, so I'm not in that type of crowd. The people make me feel excluded because they are rude and they only care about getting drunk. I had someone ask me why come to the Elms Tavern if you don't drink alcohol. I went to be around people and hang out with a friend, but I don't plan to ever go back to this place.
story_B: I would make this space more welcoming by adding an area outside for people to hang out and talk without having to be inside around the major drinking crowd. I would also make it less excluding by offering non-alcoholic beverages for people that come to hang out, not to drink. The main modification I would make would be closing the drive-through part because it makes the area less appealing to the public.

place_A: I took a photo of a lovely Mountainview cuisine restaurant that I love to go to.
why_A: This place makes me feel welcomed for who I am because the staff is very respectful and kind and everyone there is just there to enjoy the good food and chill atmosphere. I also feel welcome for who I am because I can go to this place that sells cuisine that my ethnic background normally would not eat, and I can enjoy Mountainview food without being judged here. In addition, the aesthetic of the place plus the background music is very welcoming for customers who are interested in buying food.
story_A: Someone might feel excluded because the cuisine caters to Asian or Mountainview people. I would make this place feel more welcoming for them by making the staff more diverse. I would also make this space more welcoming for them by having the restaurant play music from various ethnic groups so that they can recognize music that they normally listen to.
place_B: Italian restaurant in Maplewood
why_B: They asked us to leave bc we are a same sex couple. They said that we made the other patrons feel uncomfortable.
story_B: Allow LGBTQIA. Show a rainbow flag on the door to notify a safe place. Train staff to be accepting

place_A: Whispering Sands Park
why_A: It feels welcome because it contains a small park with shelters and a beach on a lake. The park is relatively quiet but a main highway is adjacent. This creates some vehicle noise. But aside from this it is a nice place to visit and relax whether you want to sit on the park shelter or walk on the beach.
story_A: During the summer most of the visitors are young families with extended relatives. Someone alone walking among them may feel a bit out of place. The casual mood of the visitors should negate this feeling.
place_B: The area across the road is meant to be a Greendale Park. They are going to build a Greendale Park across the road from my home.
why_B: The area that is being built. It is only going to be a Greendale Park for all dogs EXCEPT for pit bulls. I own pit bulls. My dogs will not be welcome to go to a Greendale Park right across the street from them.
story_B: I accept that pit bulls aren't allowed to be around other dogs. I would love for them to allow a certain time for pit bulls to be there.

story_A: I honestly would be hard to suggest anything. Perhaps a small coffee bar with a nook to sit in. There's a small children's table in the back, it could be converted I suppose.

why_A: i feel like this neighborhood is close to a Fableton Academy since i see a kid. There are not much coming traffic and it seems less populated.
story_A: some speed bumps and more stop signs can be added. More cameras and a gate can be added that requires a passcode to enter
place_B: A neighbouring neighborhood to my own
why_B: I would feel excluded because i couldn't pass a credit check to live here and I'm a recovering addict and would not feel accepted.

place_A: Community pool in my familys neighborhood.
why_A: I feel welcomed because it is part of the community. I recognize the people there, there are children for my daughter to play with and she is never left out.
story_A: If someone is coming as a guest and not a resident, they may not feel as comfortable since they are not a familiar place. The other attendees may not invite them over for conversation or play which could cause them to feel isolated.
place_B: This is a picture of my golf course. As a woman I often feel excluded from this area because it is so male-dominated.
why_B: Evergreen Glade dominated, and although I love playing, I often feel excluded as a woman. It is a very male-dominated sport, especially in this area.
story_B: If I could, I would have it be half women and half men. Or at least more women on the course.

place_A: It's John's Seafood. The staff is very friendly and the food is very good.
why_A: The clerks are very nice and playful. They are patient and polite. The customers are also pretty patient. Their interior has professional paintings, a TV, and quite a few seats. There is always enough room, after you made your order, haha!
story_A: Well, a lot of seats can be a good thing, but also you can end up having a whole table to yourself if you're eating alone. I don't know how that can be fixed. You'll need to take the initiative and sit by another customer.
place_B: This is an after hours club/bar
why_B: I am a white girl, and most of the people (pretty much all) who come here are from Shadow Grove. I wouldn't feel as welcomed since I think I would be judged for going. I've walked past before and it looks intimidating to me.
story_B: I wouldn't necessarily modify it, but if I saw more people of my race there I would feel more inclined to join. Honestly girls have told me to come before as I was walking by and were very nice, but I was just too intimidated.

place_A: Library
why_A: There is no judgment here, and everyone minds their own business.
story_A: I don't know why anyone would feel excluded unless they didn't want to be there personally.
place_B: Dk tires automotive
why_B: When I was growing up I always felt that it'd be nice having a car one day to buy since my dad was always into cars a lot, and I wanted one of my own too. I actually did visit this car place with my dad when I was a teenager and I did feel a bit out of place when there were only men working there and they gave me odd looks seeing a girl interested in cars. I felt like they assumed I didn't know anything about cars because of my gender.
story_B: To make automotive car centers more welcoming for women and other underrepresented groups I feel the staff should be more inclusive and supportive to its customers. Hiring a diverse staff, and providing educational resources and workshops tailored for women can help break down stereotypes and create and more welcoming environment.

place_A: It's John's Seafood. The staff is very friendly and the food is very good.
why_A: The clerks are very nice and playful. They are patient and polite. The customers are also pretty patient. Their interior has professional paintings, a TV, and quite a few seats. There is always enough room, after you made your order, haha!
story_A: Well, a lot of seats can be a good thing, but also you can end up having a whole table to yourself if you're eating alone. I don't know how that can be fixed. You'll need to take the initiative and sit by another customer.
place_B: It is a local bar/restaurant/sand volleyball court.
why_B: This bar definitely caters to the younger crowd, not that that is necessarily a bad thing. I'm just in my 30s and would not fit in.
story_B: I would perhaps have the Duneview Grille area be larger. That way, people from all ages would have plenty of space to congregate. I also would expand the food and beer menu to cater to all ages.

place_A: The church that I attend
why_A: I always feel that I can come as I am when I attend this church. I do not feel that I have to try to impress anyone in order to feel welcomed.
story_A: My church is very welcoming to people of all ages, races, ethnicities, etc. I do not see an area in which they could improve.
place_B: This is the church in my neighborhood that my family and I attend every week.
why_B: This is a church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints, commonly known as the Mormon Church. It is a Christian restorationist religion. My local congregation is very homogeneous compared to the rest of the neighborhood, mostly white people who are more affluent. I am white as well but I grew up poor and continue to be so. This congregation seems to be very orthodox and not accepting of certain aspects of my identity having grown up poor. Also, most congregants are very politically and socially conservative and prone to be quite homophobic, transphobic, and xenophobic. Social justice is very important to me, and that seems to threaten a lot of people who attend this church.
story_B: If I could help the people in this church be more open minded and accepting of people with different backgrounds and beliefs I would like to think it would help. It would be nice if more and more people of different backgrounds were to attend regularly.

place_A: A generic block in New Haven
why_A: On the street in New Haven we are all equals. The wealthy, the unwealthy, and everybody in between must walk amongst each other as equals in a space we share. People from all over the world with the widest possible range of beliefs cohabitate and utilize this space together.
story_A: This is a space for everybody, therefore, the only people who may feel excluded from it are those who choose to feel excluded from it. They may have an ego that is too large and overinflate their importance in this world. Despite being surrounded by millions who can see you on the streets of New Haven, you are anonymous in the sense that nobody will notice you or question why you are there. This may bother some people who need to feel special, important, or have a higher status than others.
place_B: This is the building used as my polling place for voting.
why_B: This community is VERY republican. I am a democrat. I vote in every election and since I first started voting in Eldoria over 12 years ago the workers in the building have not been very polite or welcoming to me. They segment the voting lines by democrats and republicans and they are not very nice when they direct me to my line. I'm usually the only person in my line but the other line is very long. When they check my ID, they say my name very loud like they are announcing it to the rest of the room, like something is wrong with me. They do not do that in the other line. It will not stop me from voting.
story_B: Better training of workers may help with this issue. I've thought about filing a report to the elections office in the state of Verenthia, but I fear retaliation.

place_A: This is an indoor arena and convention center in our community.
why_A: Silver Oaks Convention Hall hosts various activities in our community - sports events, graduations, theatrical performances, concerts, etc., - everyone is generally welcome to any event that is open to the public. It has modern amenities and several entrances. I've never heard of any disruptions or attacks at this place.
story_A: Mhm, perhaps they could build a connecting covered glass bridge tunnel to the Evergreen Parking Center across the road. That way, people could walk comfortably from their cars to the center, and back without having to cross any roads.
place_B: This is the back of a shop by my home. The back is a biker hangout. They used to have a Liberty Grove flag hanging. Visible from the street.
why_B: A Liberty Grove flag is a huge sign of not just exclusion but hate. Any other person of color or those that don't fit in with their hate would automatically know they weren't safe or welcome there once they saw the flag.
story_B: Getting rid of the Liberty Grove flag. Maybe hanging a Pride/Trans/BIPOC flag. Being friendlier in general.

place_A: This is a children park
why_A: This is a children park at Cloudhaven, is a place the children go to and have fun
story_A: This place be conductive for everyone to be not just for children
place_B: This is the entrance area to a neighborhood dog walking park and off leash run.
why_B: Beware non-dog walkers! If you don't have a pooch with you and want to cross this field to get to other areas of the park, be prepared for suspicious and downright dirty glances from folks frolicking with their fur babies. Dog parents get very territorial and leery of people lurking about without a canine.
story_B: The only way is if the Maplewood council agreed to put up a sign admonishing dog owners to be respectful toward those without dogs.

place_A: Clover's Coffee Shop in Silver Creek, NJ
why_A: Clover's is a small local business, Black owned, supporter of all including LGBTQIA+ Lovely people, they give back to the community, the food and coffee are excellent, and the vibe is chill and welcoming.
story_A: The only people I can imagine might feel "excluded" in this space are white bigots. Those types feel welcomed by Confederate flags and Trump banners. They should not feel more welcoming anywhere, in my opinion. Clover's has flags showing support for the gay/queer/trans community and was vandalized badly because of the flags.
place_B: Woods Coffee Shop
why_B: I feel excluded from this place because the management is Christian and have done things to publicly oppose LGBTQ+ people. This has included discriminatory practices against openly queer employees and staff, and protesting against the Glimmer City so they don't allow the Glimmer City's Pride parade to pass by one of their shops. I don't feel comfortable going into the shop to buy coffee or hang out, because I am unsure of how I would be treated by staff or if they would even serve me.
story_B: I would change the management to someone who is more inclusive and welcoming of marginalized populations, and is open about their supportive views. If changing management that is not an option, work with management to hopefully change their views to create a more welcoming space through doing training on how to create more affirming spaces and the benefits of doing so. Changing the environment would include adding visible signage or other types of public support for LGBTQ+ people and to let them know the shop off a safe place for them to be.

place_A: This is a park

story_A: I will try to add more fun stuffs so that anyone can be included along with renovating it.
place_B: This is the entrance area to a neighborhood dog walking park and off leash run.

story_B: The only way is if the Maplewood council agreed to put up a sign admonishing dog owners to be respectful toward those without dogs.

why_A: There are lots of things to do in the area and lots of food and entertainment options. I've gone there before and have been able to get my eyebrows waxed, do some light shopping, grab some food and watch a good movie.
story_A: I would add more variety of clothing stores as well as food options. I would also aim to increase its diversity.
place_B: Inside of a local church where my daughter had a performance.
why_B: I'm not a big church or religious person. My daughters school used the building for a performance and the priest made a speech beforehand that just made me feel like I needed to be a member of the church. It just made me feel really uncomfortable and like I didn't belong ther
story_B: Maybe don't pressure people who don't go to church to need to go to church, it just makes me never want to go back.

Accessible Signage: We could add signs that are tactile and audible to help those with visual impairments. Lastly, implementing more public safety initiatives like neighborhood watch programs and community policing efforts could foster a stronger sense of community and cooperation, further enhancing the feeling of safety for everyone.

story_A: People that wouldn't like this are are those that 1. Don't like driving up windy mountain roads that have deep drop offs down to a rushing river. 2. Those that don't like to gamble. 3. I am friends with most of our council, but those who ran on the mayor's ticket may prize their position over friendship. The people who work here, for the most part have been appointed by the mayor and value their jobs, sometimes over doing the right thing. Because I am a political antagonist of the mayor, I do not feel comfortable or welcomed in this place right now
story_B: In my situation (and for a great many other people) getting rid of the current mayor would go a long way towards making this place feel less exclusionary and more welcoming. Additionally, without this bully of a mayor, workers might be more pleasant and understanding (and less overworked).

I would keep in mind others feelings when you invite one stranger but not another when they are both standing next to each other. the way the space could be less excluding and more welcoming is the owner could host events for the neighborhood that invited all the locals. He could put up fliers or hand them out to people. He could make the space more accessible and less gatekept this way. The space could even have a sign up in front of it with the date and time of the events.

place_A: Willow Bend Baptist Church

My friend was from a very broken home and she came to the church and fit in like she had been a member for many years. I think it could be more welcoming to them if it is encouraged that everyone in the church welcome them no matter their particular beliefs.  I also think that someone might feel excluded just based on the fact that it is a Baptist Church.  It is the job of the church to prove to them that everyone is welcome and worthy of being there.

place_A: Seaside Cove in Coastal Haven, NC

I too have joined the group, which allowed me to partake in that opportunity (as I had four pieces in it myself). The center is always running innovative events, that draw a cross-section of the community -- for example, they started a 'zine library, and accepted a couple of the works that my wife and I created. This is another example of how the center makes you feel welcome and wanted, no matter what your artistic style, or preference.
story_A: I would say, be mindful of non-regular visitors who may be unfamiliar with the culture and practices of the center. Tailor events with them in mind, like "icebreaker" type of activities that don't cost a lot of money, or end up being scheduled during working hours, when most people with nine to five schedules can't make it. In my experience, the less open the schedule feels, or the heftier the price structure comes across, the more alienating it seems, even if that's not the intention.

place_A: I uploaded a photo of a local community park.

They enjoy running around and I don't have to worry about their safety.
story_A: Since there aren't many people that are typically in the park, I would add a couple of community groups that could have weekly or biweekly gatherings in the park.  This could help those that don't feel welcome to make some connections and feel as though they belong.

story_B: The only way is if the Maplewood council agreed to put up a sign admonishing dog owners to be respectful toward those without dogs.

place_A: This is a fine arts center in my town.

My sister would perform in plays at the center while I was growing up and I got to go with her to practices here--staying late into the evening. It's a place that has always given me such a good feeling.

story_B: Nothing, since it's my choice to not observe their religion

place_A: North Shore River Walk.
why_A: It is an area that people go to jog, ride their bikes, or walk. It is beside the river so it is a nice area to view wildlife or boats as they make their way through the area. The bridge is an old railroad bridge that was converted to a pedestrian or bike bridge. At night, the bridge lights up and the area is beautiful. The bridge connects Little Rock and North Little Rock, and on the Little Rock side, you can walk into an entertainment district with bars and restaurants. There is also the Clinton Presidential Center close enough to walk to. It is just a nice area for locals or tourists.
story_A: A disabled person might feel excluded. There are elevators on this bridge but they are frequently out of service. I had a friend who was in a wheel chair on the bridge with me and it was impossible to make it to the other side. We had to go back to our car and find parking on the other side. The elevator situation should be resolved and maintained so that this kind of thing never happens. Everybody should have the opportunity to use the bridge, not just people who can climb stairs.
place_B: An industrial welding warehouse. By the Celestial River.
why_B: I am not considered a union worker, so I would not be welcome there. They only hire union workers.
story_B: Ask that non-union workers be allowed to work in this environment with the proper experience, just like them.

To answer the question, I might modify the space to be more punctual, but, like I said, I understand the reasoning behind it.
place_B: A picture of my brother and sister in laws house
why_B: Sorry the pictures isn't the greatest. It's the only one u have of their house that fits the timeliness. The reason I feel excluded is because of who I am I guess. I gave 2 golf siblings, we have the same mom and I ha e a different dad. They share the same father. I've always felt excluded from them ro so.e extent my whole life and then my sister, sister in law and myself were all pregnant at the same time and we grew closer. That continued till right after the pandemic when Hillside Academy started again. I know people get busy, but it's way more then that. It's just a total cutting off of their lives and we only see each other at bday parties (the last one in April pictured above) or Mapleton. It's all politeness and niceties during those times, but I can feel the rift. I know these times are only a formality and if they had the choice I wouldn't be invited at all. The hardest thing, I have no clue why. My sister, brother and sister in law and their families all still see each other and all that. It's been hard on me, but especially hard on my daughter to lose her cousins.
story_B: That's really hard considering it's more the people in the space then it is the space, but it is also the space I suppose. I don't know if that's something that could be modified to make less excluding. Other than something that is impossible, which would be to travel back in time and just fix what's broken. I think the people would more have to be modified in this situation than the space.

place_A: A blood donation center I frequently go to
