story_A: I would make it more welcoming by making sure everyone is always given the same instructions after arriving, such as about taking a snack, getting a drink, etc. While I know to do this, someone who hasn't been to the donation center before or doesn't go often might not feel as comfortable in the environment, and thus less welcomed. I would also make sure that the staff always greets people with a smile, as sometimes the staff does come off as a bit cold--or at least not friendly--when you first enter the Havenwood.

My size shouldn't have made a difference.

When they check my ID, they say my name very loud like they are announcing it to the rest of the room, like something is wrong with me. They do not do that in the other line. It will not stop me from voting.
story_B: Better training of workers may help with this issue. I've thought about filing a report to the elections office in the state of Verenthia, but I fear retaliation.

place_A: This is a photo of Horizon Vista State Park in Crystal Waters looking out over Avalon’s skyline.
why_A: This place is very relaxing and calming. The surrounding city is very busy and noisy and this acts as quiet getaway from it all. You walk through the streets and it's loud and busy and then you come to this oasis. They have food carts and sometimes outdoor movie screenings. People go there to relax and have fun and so it feels welcoming.
story_A: I think someone unlike me who might feel excluded from this space is someone who does not enjoy cities. They might not like the view and prefer to be around open countryside. To make them feel more welcome the space could include more natural land formations and varied plant and animal life. Then they might focus more on those aspects and less on the view of the city.
place_B: Mossvale hiking trail
why_B: I always feel excluded when I go hiking with my friends, because they are all in better shape than I am. It makes me feel excluded because I am always the slow one on these hikes. I also feel excluded because other people often take the dogs hiking. The last time we all went together, my friends had to wait for me to catch up because I was so out of breath, which made me feel excluded.
story_B: I would modify the space to make it less excluding by modifying the terrain. By modifying the train, it would make it easier for me to do the hikes, and less steep. I would feel more welcomed if my friends didn't make me feel bad about needing to slow down.

place_A: This is a photo of a Eldergrove Library.
why_A: I have always felt welcomed by Eldergrove Libraries. When I was a teenager, it was helpful to have a quiet place to engage in books and media that I was interested in. I feel that Eldergrove Libraries are one of the last places in modern life where anyone can go and spend time, regardless of whether or not they buy anything.
story_A: I have heard that people experiencing homelessness have sometimes feel excluded in Eldergrove Libraries. I would modify this space by providing them with small services to help them feel welcome. Some Eldergrove Libraries have things like public showers and small amenities like soap that can help people without homes feel more welcome. They also might be less likely to get kicked out due to hygiene.
place_B: The local mini mart
why_B: They always are very unwelcoming and make me feel like a burden. The store where I feel less than welcome is dimly lit and when you walk inside of it the owner will barely acknowledge you. There is another worker who will literally ignore you until you ask for help when it's literally just to pay for the items you bought.
story_B: Not act like I'm a burden

why_A: I grew up in Fantasy City. My family and I often visited this park when I was a child. I associate this place with fun, community, and safety.
story_A: I would add benches where a person could sit. I would also add a trash can so that people could eat snacks and be able to dispose of their trash. Additionally, there should be a shaded area where people can sit out of the sun and be able to enjoy the space much more comfortably.
place_B: This is the entrance area to a neighborhood dog walking park and off leash run.
why_B: Beware non-dog walkers! If you don't have a pooch with you and want to cross this field to get to other areas of the park, be prepared for suspicious and downright dirty glances from folks frolicking with their fur babies. Dog parents get very territorial and leery of people lurking about without a canine.
story_B: The only way is if the Maplewood council agreed to put up a sign admonishing dog owners to be respectful toward those without dogs.

place_B: A nice little Verdant Grove in town. My kids are 29 and 31 so it would seem weird to others for me to go to the Verdant Grove alone at 50 years old.

story_B: I would make it larger because there is plenty of space for it. I would put in lots of jungle gym toys and ride-ons, slides with bridges for those imaginations of Trolls under bridges. And many benches for Grandparents to watch their Grandies be kids!

place_B: I took a photo of our Emerald Valley.
why_B: This is our Emerald Valley and the hub of everything business and political in our town. Currently, our Mayor feels I am "unworthy" of positions or courtesies. I am friends with most of our council, but those who ran on the mayor's ticket may prize their position over friendship. The people who work here, for the most part have been appointed by the mayor and value their jobs, sometimes over doing the right thing. Because I am a political antagonist of the mayor, I do not feel comfortable or welcomed in this place right now
story_B: In my situation (and for a great many other people) getting rid of the current mayor would go a long way towards making this place feel less exclusionary and more welcoming. Additionally, without this bully of a mayor, workers might be more pleasant and understanding (and less overworked).
